Plain language definition: Fake product sold as the genuine branded item with copied logo and packaging
Real world example: Fake Nike trainers seized at ports and sold as genuine on marketplaces
Detectable signals in listing data: Price far below recommended retail; brand in title but seller not authorised; reused or stock images; young seller account with high volume; poor description quality; brand and category mismatch

Plain language definition: Using a protected brand name to sell something that is not that brand
Real world example: Unbranded phone cases listed with iPhone and Samsung stuffed into the title for search traffic
Detectable signals in listing data: Brand terms on unbranded products; disguised brand variants such as N1ke; multiple rival brand names in one listing; brand terms hidden in description only
Detection method: Fuzzy brand matching against protected brand list

Plain language definition: Unauthorised copying of protected creative work such as images artwork or text
Real world example: Sellers printing Disney artwork on merchandise without a licence
Detectable signals in listing data: Copied listing photos across sellers; near duplicate descriptions; official marketing images on third party listings

Plain language definition: Copying the registered look and shape of a product without using the brand name
Real world example: Lookalike ride on children suitcases copying the Trunki design in the UK courts
Detectable signals in listing data: Visually similar product at undercut price; style of or inspired by language; compatible with phrasing; no brand claim
Detection method: Text similarity plus manual review queue

Plain language definition: Genuine products sold through unauthorised channels or wrong region
Real world example: Genuine perfume bought cheap abroad and resold outside the authorised distribution network
Detectable signals in listing data: Genuine brand from unauthorised seller; regional packaging references; missing warranty language; price below authorised retailers

Plain language definition: Fraudulent seller takes over or clones an established listing to ride its reputation
Real world example: Hijacked Amazon listings where a new seller wins the buy box then ships fakes
Detectable signals in listing data: Sudden seller change on established listing; sharp price drop; seller account age far younger than listing age

Plain language definition: Fake or incentivised reviews used to make fraudulent listings look trustworthy
Real world example: Review farms boosting counterfeit sellers withfive star template reviews
Detectable signals in listing data: Review velocity bursts; rating distribution skew; duplicate or template review text; review count inconsistent with account age
Detection method: Burst detection and duplicate text matching

Plain language definition: One operator running many accounts to survive takedowns
Real world example: Repeat infringers reopening under new names after enforcement
Detectable signals in listing data: Shared images addresses or templated text across sellers; synchronised listing activity; cluster of young accounts with same catalogue

Plain language definition: Unauthorised use of a patented invention or mechanism
Real world example: Gadgets copying a patented folding mechanism
Detectable signals in listing data: Largely undetectable from listing data alone; flag lookalike functional products for expert review
Detection method: Out of scope; manual referral only
